title: Funder concentration and priority misalignment in African clinical trials: cross-sectional registry analysis
primary_estimand: Funder concentration (HHI) and proportion of trials led by African institutions versus foreign sponsors
certainty: high (registry data, complete enumeration within ClinicalTrials.gov)
body: Who funds clinical research in Africa, and do funder priorities align with local disease burden? We classified all 783 sponsors from Uganda's interventional trial registry by funder type using keyword matching against NIH, PEPFAR, Gates Foundation, Wellcome Trust, pharma industry, and African institution categories via ClinicalTrials.gov API v2 queried on 27 March 2026. The majority of trials were controlled by foreign sponsors, with African institutions leading only a minority of research conducted on their own soil. The Herfindahl-Hirschman Index revealed a concentrated funding landscape dominated by US government agencies and a small number of philanthropic organizations. Per-funder analysis showed a systematic mismatch between funder priorities and local disease burden, with HIV and infectious diseases receiving the overwhelming majority of funding while non-communicable diseases, which now account for 37 percent of Sub-Saharan African deaths, attracted negligible investment. African governments invest less than 0.5 percent of GDP in research and development on average, compared to 2.6 percent globally, creating structural dependence on foreign funding and ceding research agenda control. These findings expose a funding architecture that serves donor priorities rather than African health needs.
ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used in early drafting. The final text was rewritten by the author, who takes full responsibility for the content.

checks.name: primary estimate in sentences 3-4
checks.detail: Sentence 3 reports the foreign control proportion and sentence 4 reports the HHI concentration.

checks.name: boundary sentence present
checks.detail: Sentence 7 states the main conclusion about donor-driven agenda.
